coredata.dc:title: Lignin-derived carbon quantum dots/Ni-MOL heterojunction from red phosphorus-assisted ball milling pretreatment and their application in photocatalysis: An insight from experiment and DFT calculation

coredata.dc:description: The constructing defects by doping phosphorus elements are limited by high energy consumption, environment pollution and long preparation time. In this work, lignin-derived carbon quantum dots/nickel metal-organic layer (Ni-MOL) heterojunction was successfully prepared by red phosphorus-assisted ball milling pretreatment. And the application in photocatalytic tetracycline (TC) degradation have been investigated systematically by the combination of the calculational and experimental methods. The maximum degradation rate was 98.98% for P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL in 120 min under visible light condition. P-doping was beneficial to reduce the electron transport resistance. Moreover, it increased the hydrophilicity but reduced the position of conduction band, thus more active •O2 - for TC degradation were generated. Hence, the novel photocatalyst prepared by the simple and low energy consumption method is expected to treat TC wastewater effectively and this work makes a contribution to the perfection of P-doped photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The constructing defects by doping phosphorus elements are limited by high energy consumption, environment pollution and long preparation time. In this work, lignin-derived carbon quantum dots/nickel metal-organic layer (Ni-MOL) heterojunction was successfully prepared by red phosphorus-assisted ball milling pretreatment. And the application in photocatalytic tetracycline (TC) degradation have been investigated systematically by the combination of the calculational and experimental methods. The maximum degradation rate was 98.98% for P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL in 120 min under visible light condition. P-doping was beneficial to reduce the electron transport resistance. Moreover, it increased the hydrophilicity but reduced the position of conduction band, thus more active •O2 - for TC degradation were generated. Hence, the novel photocatalyst prepared by the simple and low energy consumption method is expected to treat TC wastewater effectively and this work makes a contribution to the perfection of P-doped photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been widely applied in medical treatment (Gothwal et al., 2015). Low levels of antibiotic residues may generate antibiotic-resistant bacteria, and thus they could menace human health (Yang et al., 2018a; Ahlawat et al., 2020). However, conventional water treatment techniques can not effectively remove them due to their stable chemical structures and recalcitrance to bio-degradation. Developing new techniques to thoroughly remove antibiotics from water is crucial in this endeavor.

Photocatalytic, as one of the advanced oxidation technologies, has attracted more and more concern in antibiotic degradation because it is environmental-friendly, highly stable and cheap (Li et al., 2016). Plenty of photocatalysts are used to degrade antibiotics, such as TiO2, g-C3N4, BiOX (XF, Cl, Br and I), CdS and metal organic frameworks (MOFs) (Lin et al., 2021). As a new type of photocatalyst, MOFs have received sufficient attention in the photocatalysis field because of large specific surface area, abundant accessible active sites, tunable organic linkers/metal nodes and high porosity, of which are beneficial to degrade antibiotics (Li et al., 2019). For instance, ZnMgAl layered triple hydroxide MOFs showed outstanding photocatalytic for organic dye (Bhuvaneswari et al., 2021). A new bifunctional 2D Cd/Co-based MOFs could act as an efficient photocatalyst to degrade organic pollutants (Yi et al., 2018). The photocatalyst heterostructure constructed by In2Se3 and MOFs exhibited excellent photodegradation performance for tetracycline (Li et al., 2022). Our previous study found that the combination of carbon quantum dots (CD) and MOFs (CD/MOFs) can further improve the catalytic performance for antibiotics (the degradation performance of tetracycline (TC) was improved by 237%). However, the recombination rate of photogenerated holes for CD/MOFs photocatalysts is high, which is not benefit for the improvement of photocatalytic performance.

To solve this problem, many researchers focused on the separation of photoelectron-hole. Constructing defects in photocatalysts can effectively avoid the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole. The constructing defects by doping non-metallic elements has attracted sufficient attention as it is simplicity and low cost. The non-metallic elements of nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), Boron (B) and phosphorus (P) have been used to form defects (Selvakumar et al., 2022; Song et al., 2019). Among those elements, P has the largest atomic radius, resulting in the largest substitution defect. The bond length of C-P is obviously larger than that of C-X (XN, S, B), leading to a larger structural deformation of the photocatalyst. In terms of electronegativity, N (3.04) and S (2.58) are more electronegative than carbon (2.55), while P (2.19) is less electronegative than carbon (Yashwanth et al., 2022). The polarity of C-P bond is opposite to that of C-N and C-S bond. Therefore, P doped can generate more defect sites and new active sites, of which is different from N and S doped (Wei et al., 2022). It will show great potential for P-doped modified CD/MOFs photocatalysts.

Phosphoric acid, sodium dihydrogen phosphate and phosphorus tribromide were used in P-doped CD/MOFs photocatalysts. However, the problems of low P content and uneven distribution of P in CD/MOFs photocatalysts have not been solved yet. Triphenylphosphine can effectively modify CD/MOFs photocatalysts by forming firm C-P bond, and it is the best P precursor discovered so far. But the application is limited by the toxicity and high price. To our knowledge, no literature reported red phosphrous (RP) as a P source to modify MOFs composite. Compared with other P sources, RP has the advantages of non-toxic, non-corrosive and low cost. The methods of P doping CD/MOFs photocatalysts include hydrothermal, high temperature pyrolysis, the combination of impregnation and high temperature, the combination of sol-gel and high temperature treatment, high temperature and high pressure method, etc (Qiao et al., 2019). The preparation cycles of these methods are long and the P utilization rate is low. To our knowledge, the method of mechanical ball milling has not been used to doping CD/MOFs photocatalysts. Mechanical ball milling makes full use of the impact of ball grinding medium, cutting and filling effect of the material to undergo repeated collision, compression and fracture process, resulting in the diffusion between the materials on the micro scale (Sakthivel et al., 2020).

In this work, RP as P source was chosen to modify CD/MOFs photocatalysts by ball milling. Nickel metal-organic layer (Ni-MOL) was selected as 2D MOFs to support CD and constructed a layered composite photocatalyst. The prepared layered composite photocatalyst exhibited high photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of TC, a typical antibiotic. In addition, the charge transfer and TC photodegradation mechanisms of the layered composite photocatalyst were also investigated.
All chemical reagents used in this work are supplied in Supporting information.
The sample was prepared by ball milling and hydrothermal method. The schematic diagram of synthetic route was shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of P-modified carbon quantum dots (P-CD)
ce:section.ce:para: 3.0 g lignin and a certain amount (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 g) of RP were first added into ball mill tank. Then 5 mL ethanol and ZrO2 balls were also added to the ball mill. After 8 h of ball milling at 600 rpm, 2.0 g of the obtained powder and 0.3 g NaOH were added into 50 mL aqueous solution, followed by heated up at 180 ℃ for 5 h in the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (Sakthivel et al., 2020). The suspension was centrifuged for 5 min at 10,000 r/pm. The liquids (P-CD) were preserved for later use, and named P(0)-CD, P(0.5)-CD, P(1.0)-CD, P(1.5)-CD and P(2.0)-CD, respectively, according to the amounts of RP added.

Solution A was prepared with adding P-phthalic acid (0.166 g) into 5 mL DMF solution and stirred for 10 min solution B was prepared with adding Ni(NO3)2⋅6 H2O (0.436 g) into 10 mL DMF solution and stirred for 10 min. Later, solution A was drop-wise added into solution B to form solution C. Then 2 mL NaOH (0.04 mol·L−1) was slowly added into solution C. The mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated up at 120 ℃ for 10 h. After filtration, the precipitate was collected and washed with DMF and alcohol for several times, followed by oven-drying at 50 ℃ for 12 h. Then, Ni-MOL was synthesized (Li et al., 2022).

1.0 g Ni-MOL was added into 50 mL P-CD solutions. The mixture was then transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated up at 120 ℃ for 10 h (Sharma et al., 2020). After cooling down naturally, the precipitate was collected by filtration and washed with water and alcohol for several times, followed by vacuum oven-drying at 60 ℃ for 12 h to get the final product, P-CD/Ni-MOL. According to the amounts of RP added, they were named as P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(0.5)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(1.5)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(2.0)-CD/Ni-MOL, respectively.

The crystal structure and morphologies of photocatalysts were observed via scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS SUPRA 55, Germany) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2F20, USA). Surface profile and roughness of catalysts were performed with atomic force microscope (AFM, MFP-3D infinity). The crystal phase analysis of photocatalyst was collected on X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was undertaken by FTIR spectrometer (IR Affinity-1 s, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) analysis was carried on UV-2600 UV–VIS spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) with a wavelength region of 200–800 nm. The chemical composition and states of catalysts were determined by X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) via ESCALABXi + XPS System (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Photoluminescence spectra (PL) was obtained from the Edinburgh FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Photocatalytic reaction of the light source from 300 W Xe lamp (CEL-LAM500) with a filter, a light source, with the wavelength range of 400–780 nm was provided for the reaction. The photocurrent, EIS and Mott-Schottky curve were recorded with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, China). High performance liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS, Shimadzu LCMS-010) was used for the qualitative analysis of the intermediates. Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (3D EEMs, Hitachi F7000) were employed to further grasp the behaviors of TC molecules in the process of degradation reaction. Total organic carbon (TOC) was detected by total organic carbon analyzer (TOC, Shimadzu CN-200). The solid state 31P NMR was recorded by Bruker AMX 300 spectrometer. The contact angle was tested by LAUDA Scientific (LSA 60). And the particle size of CD was recorded by laser particle analyzer(Malvern, Mastersizer 2000).

Photocatalytic degradation of TC was carried out using a Xenon lamp (400–780 nm, Beijing China Education Au-light CO., Ltd) as light source. Optical power density measured at a distance from the light source was 15 mW·cm−2.

To test the catalytic ability, the prepared catalyst (30.0 mg) was added into TC solution (100 mg·L−1, 30 mL) under magnetic stirring. The suspension was placed in dark to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium before irradiation. Then it was exposed to the light mentioned before. At given time intervals, the concentrations of TC remaining in the solution were measured.

In the active species trap experiments, the concentration of TC was 270 μM. P-Benzoquinone (BQ, 92.5 mM) and EDTA disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA-2Na, 80.6 mM) were acted as scavengers of superoxide radical (•O2-) and hole (h+), respectively, while isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 7.5 mM) and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 76.5 mM) were served as scavenger of hydroxyl radical (•OH) and electron (e-), respectively.

All density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out with the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) (Kresse et al., 1996). The electron exchange-correlation effect and electron-ion cores interactions were treated with the Perdew−Burke−Ernzerhof (PBE) functional and the projected-augmented wave (PAW) methods, respectively (Perdew et al., 1996). The energy cutoff of plane-wave was set to 400 eV. A 1 × 1 × 1 Γ-centered Monhorst-Pack k-point mesh was employed for all calculations. The weak van der Waals interactions were descriped with Grimme’s DFT-D3 approach (Grimme et al., 2010). The carbon quantum dots were built with the graphite unit cell with space group of P63 mc. A 15 Å vacuum were added in all three spatial directions in order to eliminate the interactions between QD images. The formation energies (

Fig. 2 shows the SEM images of Ni-MOL and P-CD/Ni-MOL. Ni-MOL displayed morphology of ultrathin layers and the surface was relatively smooth (Fig. 2(a)). Compared with Ni-MOL, the diameter of P-CD/Ni-MOL became smaller and the surface became more roughly after loading P-CD (Fig. 2(b)). In addition, the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) result proved that C, O, Ni and P existed in P-CD/Ni-MOL, suggesting that P modified CD have successfully loaded at Ni-MOL (Fig. 2(c)).

Fig. 3 shows the TEM and the HRTEM images of Ni-MOL and P-CD/Ni-MOL. As shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b), Ni-MOL was 2D sheet-like structure and lattice fringes with a width of 0.31 nm, corresponding to the crystal planes of (100) of Ni-MOL. Furthermore, the SAED pattern indicated that Ni-MOL was pure monocrystal (inset of Fig. 3(a)). The TEM (Fig. 3(c)) and the HRTEM (Fig. 3(d)) images of P-CD/Ni-MOL showed that P-CD/Ni-MOL samples were lamellar structure and plenty of CDs had loaded at Ni-MOL. Crystal lattice (0.36 and 0.31 nm, Fig. 3(d)) were attributed to CD (110) and Ni-MOL (100), respectively, further confirming that the incorporation of Ni-MOL and CD was successful. Moreover, EDS mapping verified that the C, O, Ni, and P elements were uniformly distributed (Fig. 3(e)), suggesting that CD was uniformly distributed in Ni-MOL rather than clustering together. Fig. 3(f) and (g) show AFM images of Ni-MOL and P-CD/Ni-MOL. The surface of Ni-MOL was smooth in consistent with SEM result. After loading CD, the surface of P-CD/Ni-MOL became rough and the irregular sphere particles could be clearly observed. The size of sphere particle (Fig.S1) was approximately 3–5 nm, which was consistent with the size of CD (Fig.S2), suggesting that CD was semi-embedded in the outer surface of Ni-MOL.

XRD pattern of CD, Ni-MOL and P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL (X = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2) are shown in Fig. 4(a). Peaks located at 8.5° and 16.9° were corresponding to (010) and (100) crystalline planes of Ni-MOL phase structure (Yang et al., 2021a). For CDs, the diﬀraction peak at 2θ = 8.1°, 22.9° and 31.6° were derived from the interlayer reﬂection of a graphite-like CDs crystal structure (Yang et al., 2021b; Ashritha et al., 2021). The characteristic peaks of Ni-MOL and CDs were found in P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL (X = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2), suggesting that CD had loaded in Ni-MOL. No other phases could be detected in P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL samples, indicating that the P element was highly dispersed and did not change the crystal structure of CD/Ni-MOL. Increasing the amounts of P in CDs, the peak located at 31.6° migrated to lower diffraction angle, suggesting that P entered the CD as doping, rather than forming other new substances (Zhang et al., 2021a).

Fig. 4(b) shows the FT-IR spectra of Ni-MOL, and P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL (X = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2). Peaks sited at 3467 cm−1 were corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O-H of the analyzed samples (Ahmad et al., 2015, 2017). Series peaks located at 1413–1668 cm−1 related to the feature peaks of benzene rings and carbonyl (Li et al., 2022). Peak appearing at 1210 cm−1 belonged to the feature peak of C-O. After introducing P in CD/Ni-MOL, new peaks located at 1180 cm−1 in P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL (X = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2) were found, which were corresponding to the stretching vibrations of P-O (Puziy et al., 2002). In addition, peak appeared at 723 cm−1 in P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL (X = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2) was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C-P. Increasing the amounts of P in CDs, the intensity of absorption peak located at 723 cm−1 increased, indicating that P entered into the crystal lattice of CD rather than being as the adsorbed state of PO4 3- (Li et al., 2013).

In order to further investigate the different formation of P, XPS spectra of Ni-MOL, CD/Ni-MOL and P-CD/Ni-MOL were recorded ( Fig. 5). All peaks of the elements of Ni-MOL and CD/Ni-MOL could be found in P-CD/Ni-MOL. Compared with Ni-MOL and CD/Ni-MOL, new peak located at 133 eV in P-CD/Ni-MOL (Fig. 5(a) inset) could be observed, indicating that P had successfully loaded on CD/Ni-MOL. C 1 s spectrum of Ni-MOL, CD/Ni-MOL and P-CD/Ni-MOL are shown in Fig. 5(b). For Ni-MOL, peaks located at 284.06 eV and 287.76 eV could be attributed to two different carbon states, assigning to various carbon C-C(sp2) and OC-O (Qu et al., 2020). After combining with CD, the peak shape was greatly changed. Peak located at 285.63 eV was ascribed to C-O-R (R is aliphatic hydrocarbon). As the main forms of C in lignin were Ph-O-R and C-O-R, the content of C-O-R increased due to benzene ring fracture resulted from high-speed ball milling and hydrothermal alkali erosion (Liu et al., 2021). The appearance of peak C-O-R in CD/Ni-MOL indicated that CD had successfully loaded on Ni-MOL. After doping P in CD/Ni-MOL, both C-O-R and C-C peaks shifted 0.3 eV. The spectrum of O 1 s are exhibited in Fig. 5(c). Peaks at 530.84 eV and 532.08 eV in Ni-MOL were corresponding to the Ni-O and benzene carbonyl, respectively. For CD/Ni-MOL, peak at 531.50 eV was ascribed to Ni-O, and peak at 532.71 eV was corresponding to benzene carbonyl and CO and C-O-C. For P-CD/Ni-MOL, peak located at 532.74 eV was ascribed to CO/C-O-C/C-O-P. Peaks at 531.30 and 531.99 eV were attributed to Ni-O and PO (Yang et al., 2018b). Compared with CD/Ni-MOL, the peak area of P-CD/Ni-MOL was significantly changed especially in 532.74 eV, suggesting the content of C-O-P was increased. Fig. 5(d) shows the P 2p spectrum. There was no P existence in CD/Ni-MOL. Peaks located at 133.94, 133.45 and 132.87 eV in P-CD/Ni-MOL were corresponding to C-O-P, C-P-O and C-PO, respectively (Gao et al., 2017). The spectrum of Ni was exhibited in Fig. 5(e). Peaks appeared at 878.78, 873.08, 860.81, and 855.44 eV were assigned to Ni 2p1/2 sat, Ni 2p1/2, Ni 2p3/2 sat and Ni 2p3/2, respectively. The spin–orbit separations of Ni was 24.04 eV, indicating that Ni was appeared as Ni2+ in P-CD/Ni-MOL.

Fig. 6(a) shows solid-state 31P NMR spectra of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL. No signal located at around 0 ppm could be observed, suggesting no phosphate-like structure in P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL. It suggested that P enterred into the lignin-based carbon rather than being as the adsorbed state of PO4 3-, which was consistent with the previous XRD results. The dominant signal (around −65 ppm) contributed to C-PO bond (Sun et al., 2015). A resonance signal centered at 17 ppm was ascribed to phosphates, compounds with C-P bonding (Puziy et al., 2008). Peaks located at 78 and 112 ppm were attributed to P which combined with benzyl and phenol, indicating that P had successfully infiltrated lignin-based CD (Strelko et al., 1999). The fourth peak at − 163 ppm was most likely ascribed to C-O-P bond (Fu et al., 2003). Based on the peak area formed by different forms of P, the content order of P forms was C-PO > C-P > ph-O-P > ph-C-P > C-O-P. To sum up, the order of P form content was C-PO > C-O-P > C-P-O, which was in agreement with the results of XPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalysis performance for TC under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of different samples under visible light irradiation are shown in Fig. 6(b). No significant change could be found in TC concentration for Ni-MOL. However, after loading CD on Ni-MOL, the removal rate of TC by P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL had reached 47.69%. The enhancement of photocatalytic performance might be attributed to two facts. The first fact was that Ni-MOL with wide band gap could not effectively absorb visible light. Carbon quantum dots had the up-conversion luminescence property, which could effectively change the light absorption range of composite photocatalyst. The second fact was that CD could act as both electron donor and acceptor, and act as carrier transfer bridge in the composite catalyst, which could effectively reduce the electron-hole pair recombination rate. Photocatalytic performance was further improved after doping P on the surface of CD/Ni-MOL. In addition, the samples with different P doping amounts exhibited different photocatalytic performance. The order was P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL>P(1.5)-CD/Ni-MOL>P(0.5)-CD/Ni-MOL>P(2.0)-CD/Ni-MOL. The maximum degradation rate was 98.98% for P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL. Fig. 6(c) shows the photocatalytic performance of different samples under ultraviolet light irradiation. The photocatalytic performance of Ni-MOL was greatly improved when the light was converted from visible light to ultraviolet light. On the contrary, the photocatalytic performance of P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL (X = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2) decreased significantly when visible light was converted to ultraviolet light, indicating the load of P-CDs could adjust the band gap of the composite material. The removal rate of P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL (X = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2) under ultraviolet light can be attributed to the adsorption performance of these materials. As we know, the defects could be constructed by doping P, which could effectively prevent photoelectron-hole recombination (Vithal et al., 2013). Theoretically speaking, the more P doped, the more defects formed, the better photocatalytic performance should be. However, the optimal p-doping amount was 1.0 g rather than 2.0 g. The reason for this was that excess P would form a new electron-hole complex center, which was not conducive to the improvement of photocatalytic performance (Zhang et al., 2021b).

The synergistic effect of light absorption, charge separation and transport, and surface reaction would determine the photocatalytic performance of photocatalyst (Xiao et al., 2018). The photocatalytic performance can be estimated by the following formula:
(2) ηPP = ηLA × ηCST × ηSR

where η
PP is the photocatalytic performance, η
LA is the light absorption efficiency, η
CST is the charge separation and transport efficiency, and η
SR is the surface reaction efficiency.

The first step of photocatalytic reaction was the absorption of solar energy. The evaluation index was light absorption efficiency (η
LA ). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) was used to investigate the light absorption. Fig. 7(a) shows the UV–vis DRS of Ni-MOL, P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL. Based on the Kubelka-Munk formula, the calculated band gap energy of Ni-MOL, P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL were 3.52, 2.09, and 2.23 eV, respectively (Fig.S3) (Khatoon et al., 2018). The band gap of Ni-MOL decreased by 1.29 eV after loading CD, due to the fact that CD could be stimulated partially by visible and near-infrared light. Low-energy visible light could be converted to high-energy visible light through the upconversion effect (Shen et al., 2012). The luminescence of CD was utilized by coupling of Ni-MOL with CD, leading to the narrowing the band gap and broadening of light absorption range (Gokus et al., 2009). In addition, P-doping could also alter the band gap. Compared with P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, the band gap of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL had increased by 0.14 eV, which further broadened the absorption of visible light.

The separation and conduction of photon-generated carrier plays a vital role in photocatalysis. Hence, the η
CST of samples were characterized by various experiments and devices. Fig. 7(b) shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CD, P-CD, Ni-MOL, P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL. The absorption wavelength of CD and P-CD are located at 750 nm, which is consistent with the property of near-infrared luminescence (Cui et al., 2022). The absorption wavelength of P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL red-shifted at 440 nm, which could be attributed to the fact that the combination of CD and Ni-MOL could reduce the band gap. The fluorescence intensity of P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL were significantly less than that of Ni-MOL (Fig. 7(b) inset), indicating that P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL had a lower electron-hole recombination ratio. The absorption wavelength at 654 and 719 nm disappeared in comparison with PL spectra of Ni-MOL, due to the fact that CD had the property of up-conversion luminescence, which could directly capture near-infrared light and convert it into visible light. Compared with P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, the fluorescence intensity of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL had reduced, suggesting that P-doping could reduce the electron-hole recombination rate. Fig. S4 shows the representative time-resolved luminescence spectra of Ni-MOL, P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL. The PL lifetime of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL was 0.421628 ns, but the PL lifetime of the Ni-MOL and P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL was only about 0.138526 ns and 0.183564 ns, respectively. This indicated that P-doping was benefit for the separation of photogenerated electrons-holes.

Fig. 7(c) and (d) show the transient photocurrent density and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plot of Ni-MOL, P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL. The transient photocurrent of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL was much higher than that of Ni-MOL and P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL. The diameters of arc radius for P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL was smaller than that of other samples, indicating a stronger separation ability of photo-generated carriers and smaller transmission resistance. Compared with the transient photocurrent of Ni-MOL and P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, the transient photocurrent of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL was higher, suggesting that the P-doped could accelerate the separation of photo-generated carriers. To further investigate the impact of P on CD/Ni-MOL, different ratios of P (0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 g) in CD/Ni-MOL were designed. Fig. 8(a) shows electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of P(X)-CD/Ni-MOL (X = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2). Increasing the content of P-doped, the curvature radius of photocatalyst decreased, indicating that P-doping decreased the charge transfer resistance of the CD/Ni-MOL samples and higher doping amount lead to a lower charge transfer resistance (Wu et al., 2020). This is due to the fact that P was doped into the carbon lattice and replaced C and O in the process of ball milling, forming the bond of C-P or C-P-O. Because the electronegativity of P (2.19) is less than that of C (2.55) and O (3.44), the binding capacity of P to electrons was weaker than that of C and O, resulting in the reduction of electron transfer resistance (Wang et al., 2014). Theoretical calculations showed that the P doped material had smaller minimum energy required for an electron to escape from the surface of photocatalyst (Zhang et al., 2020a; Cao et al., 2022). Fig. 8(b) shows stable and invertible photocurrent responses in four light on–off cycles under visible light irradiation. It is interesting that the higher doping amount did not result in the stronger photocurrent density. The photocurrent strength order were P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL>P(1.5)-CD/Ni-MOL>P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL>P(2.0)-CD/Ni-MOL>P(0.5)-CD/Ni-MOL, which was not similar to the trend of EIS. This might be due to the fact that different P configurations were formed by different P addition, and different P configurations had different abilities in separating photogenerated electrons and holes.

Fig. 8(c) shows Mott-Schottky curves of different content P-doped samples ((P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(0.5)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(1.5)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(2.0)-CD/Ni-MOL)). According to the M-S curve, the flat band potential of (P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(0.5)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL, P(1.5)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(2.0)-CD/Ni-MOL were 0.887, 0.397, 0.427, 0.497 and 0.637 (V vs NHE), respectively. It is noteworthy that the semiconductor type of CD/Ni-MOL had changed from P type to N type after doping P in CD/Ni-MOL. This further verified that the binding ability of materials to electrons was weakened after P entered the carbon lattice, which was conducive to electron transfer. In addition, the conduction band position of the photocatalyst became more negative with the increase of P doping. In other words, as the amount of P doping increased, the greater the barrier between the band edge and the reaction potential was, the greater the driving force of carrier movement was, and the faster the formation rate of peroxic radicals were (Bi et al., 2019).

The surface reaction was the last step in the photocatalytic process, which could directly influence the photocatalytic performance. η
SR were decided by the specific surface area, the adsorption and activation of water, and the production of active radical species. Based on the results of BET, the specific surface area of Ni-MOL, P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL were 314.67, 298.68 and 305.19 m2·g−1, respectively. The specific surface areas of P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL were lower than that of Ni-MOL, due to the fact that CD had blocked the internal pores of Ni-MOL, resulting in a reduction in specific surface area. Compared with P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, the specific surface area of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL was slightly increased, indicating P-doping offered more active sites on the surface of photocatalyst. Afterwards, water contact angle was used to explore the adsorption capacity of photocatalyst towards water. The water contact angle of Ni-MOL, P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL are shown in Fig. 9. The contact angle of Ni-MOL, P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL were 44.3°, 32.5° and 21.7°, respectively. Compared with Ni-MOL, the contact angle P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL was decreased. It is based on the fact that the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL increased and the surface became convex and uneven (could be proved by AFM image) after doping CD in Ni-MOL, resulting in the decrease in contact angle. The contact angle of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL decreased further than that of P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL, because P entered into lignin and replaced carbon to form C-P-O or C-PO in the process of ball milling (could be proved by XPS and 31P NMR), thus further enhancing the hydrophilicity. A smaller contact angle meant less resistance in the formation of free radicals with water involved. In addition, it is proved that the adsorption capacity of the photocatalyst surface to oxygen was enhanced after doping P (Cao et al., 2022).

In order to further investigate the configuration of C-PO, C-O-P and C-P-O, calculations were used to explore the substitutional position of P atom. Fig. S5 shows the possible P-substituted configurations. The formation energy (Eform) of possible P-substituted configurations are shown in Table 1. From the point of view of forming energy, the most stable configuration of C-O-P, C-P-O and C-PO were corresponding to the configuration number of 4, 7 and 9, respectively.

Moreover, the impacts of P-doping were investigated from DFT calculation by density of states and charge difference distribution. Fig. 10(a) reveals C-O-P, C-P-O and C-PO configurations. Fig. 10(b) shows the partial density of states (PDOS) of C-O-P, C-P-O and C-PO configuration. C-O-P was contributed to the formation of conduction band minimum (CBM), while C-P-O and C-PO had no effect on the formation of CBM (Cui et al., 2022). The electron transfer method of C-O-P, C-P-O and C-PO configuration at the atomic level were described by charge difference distribution (CHG) (Fig. 10(c)). The C-O-P, C-P-O and C-PO configuration had different electron transport channels. For C-O-P and C-PO configuration, the electron accumulated in the defect of C and O, with depletion in P. In other words, the electrons produced by photocatalysis were transferred to the outside via P. Compared with C-O-P and C-PO configuration, the electron depletion of C-P-O configuration existed on P and O. Empirically, P atom is lower than carbon and oxygen at electronegativity, resulting in the highest electron cloud density. The CHG results were consistent with our previous study. This result indicated that P-doping could result in charge redistribution and then boost local charge separation and electron transfer, which improved the photocatalytic efficiency (Liu et al., 2018).

To further investigate the impacts of active radical species on photocatalytic mechanism, trapping experiments were designed to detect the active radical species in photocatalytic reaction. p-BQ, EDTA-2Na, IPA, and K2Cr2O7 were acted as the scavengers for•O2 -, h+, •OH, and e-, respectively. Fig. S6 shows the change of degradation efficiency with adding scavengers. Compared with adding no scavenger, no significant change could be found in degradation efficiency with adding EDTA. After adding K2Cr2O7 and IPA scavenger, the degradation efficiency partially decreased. However, compared with adding no scavenger, an obvious decrease in degradation efficiency could be found with adding p-BQ. The results of trapping experiments indicated that TC molecular were mainly attacked by •O2 -. The results of M-S proved P(0)-CD/Ni-MOL a P-type semiconductor. After doping P, P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL had changed from P-type to N-type, indicating that the number of e- was more than h+ in the carriers. According to the theory of reactive free radical formation, e- was involved in the formation of •O2 - and h+ was invited to participate the formation of •OH (Wang et al., 2014). Based on the results of EIS, the electron transport resistance decreased after doping P. Hence, the number of •O2 - was more than •OH and h+ in attacking TC molecules.

Based on above analysis, the plausible enhanced photocatalytic mechanism could be proposed ( Fig. 11). In the ball milling process, P entered into the lignin-based CD to form C-P-O, C-PO and C-O-P configurations. P doping modification resulted in the photocatalyst changing from P type to N type and the reduction of electron transfer resistance, which was more conducive to electron transfer. P-doping also changed the band gap width of the photocatalyst and enhanced the barrier between the band edge and the reaction potential. C-O-P configuration was contributed to the formation of new energy band. P could be used as a photocatalyst for electron transport terminals. In addition, P-doping modification could improve the hydrophilicity of the photocatalyst. Hence, P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL exhibited higher photodegradation performance towards TC.

According to the aforementioned results, the degradation mechanism of TC by P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL could be summarized. The suitable light to energy gap was adsorbed by P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL and inspired the electron to excite from the valence band (VB) to the conduction bond (CB). At the same time, the vacancy of the electron in the VB was corresponding to the positive hole. Hence, photo-generated electron-hole pairs were produced (2). The generated electron was transferred to O2 through terminals C-O-P, C-P-O and C-PO. •O2 - was produced by O2 taking electron (3). The combination of •O2 - and H2O could produce HO2• and OH-. The generated h+ located at VB was captured by OH- and transformed into •OH (5). The TC molecules were attacked by •O2 -, •OH and h+ VB, and degraded into degradation products or to CO2 and H2O (6).
(2) Photocatalyst + hy→eˉCB + h+ VB
(3) O2 + eˉCB →•O2ˉ
(4) •O2ˉ+H2O→HO2• + OH-
(5) OH-+h+ VB→•OH
(6)
•O2ˉ+ h+ VB +•OH +tetracycline compound→Degradation products or CO2 +H2O

Fig. 12 shows three-dimensional excitation emission matrix (3D EMM) spectrum of different photodegradation stages (S1: collected without irradiation; S2: taken after 30 min irradiation; S3: taken after 60 min irradiation; and S4: taken after 120 min irradiation). As shown in Fig. 12(a), there was no ﬂuorescence signal for the sample without irradiation, indicating that TC molecules were not destroyed. After 30 min irradiation, the peaks located at I and II areas were detected (Fig. 12(b)), suggesting that TC molecules were broken into serials tyrosine and tryptophan molecules (Zhu et al., 2022). With increases of illumination time, the contents of serials tyrosine and tryptophan molecules were increased (Fig. 12(c)). In addition, signals located at IV areas could be observed, clarifying that serials tyrosine and tryptophan molecules were further degraded into small soluble molecules (Valencia et al., 2014). After 120 min irradiation, the content of soluble molecules was increased and the intermediate as a whole was transformed from a large molecule to a small molecule (Coble et al., 1996).

To further explore the formation mechanism of the serial tyrosine and tryptophan molecules fluorescence region, and the degradation pathway of TC, high performance liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) was used to detect the intermediate products at different illumination time. The m/z values of 475, 460, 444, 401, 340, 327, 292, 252, 175, 164, 139, 136, and 90 were detected. According to the results of 3D EMM, trapping experiments, theoretical speculation and HPLC-MS, the possible degradation pathways of TC were proposed. The chemical structure and possible degradation paths of TC are shown in Fig. 13. Based on the molecule character of TC and Fukui index, the ring D, C1, C5a, C6, C14 and N of TC showed high tendency to be attacked by reactive oxygen species (Li et al., 2020a; Zhang et al., 2020b). Therefore, TC molecules were firstly attacked by •O2 – radicals on the C11a and C3 sites and P1(m/z = 444) was generated (Cao et al., 2019). Two hydroxyl groups located at C3 were translated into carbonyl groups. Subsequently, C11a was further attacked by •O2 – radicals and the intermediate product with m/z value of P2 (m/z = 460) was produced (Wu et al., 2021). After deimination and deamidation reaction, P2 (m/z = 460) had been oxidized into P3 (m/z = 340) by •OH. At last, P3 (m/z = 340) was produced by N, N-demethylation and dehydroxylation, and was translated into P4 (m/z = 136) and P5 (m/z = 175) by h+ after ring opening reaction (Wu et al., 2021).

In path way II, TC molecules were firstly attacked by •OH and changed into P6 (m/z = 401) via decarbonylation at N1 and the dehydroxylation at C6 (Liu et al., 2020). P6 (m/z = 401) could produce intermediate product P3 (m/z = 340) by losing an amino group (site N1) and an acetamide (site N2) under the attack of the hydroxyl radical. Afterwards, P7 (m/z = 327) was generated via dehydroxylation at C2, dehydrogenation at C5a, hydroxylation at C17 (Huang et al., 2020). After ring opening reaction, P7 (m/z = 327) was converted into P8 (m/z = 252). Finally, the intermediate products P8 (m/z = 252) further was attacked by h+ and •OH, and cracked into P9 (m/z = 139) after suffering ring opening, hydroxyl radical oxidation and structural rearrangement.

In path III, TC were firstly attacked by •OH, generating an intermediate product P10 (m/z = 475) after the addition of carbonyl and aldehyde groups (Li et al., 2017). Subsequently, P10 (m/z = 475) was assaulted by h+, •OH and •O2 -, producing intermediate product P11 (m/z = 292) by withstanding ring opening, detachment of methyl and hydroxyl groups. Finally, P12 (m/z = 164), P4 (m/z = 136) and P13 (m/z = 90) were gained via further fragmentation of P11 (m/z = 292) by •OH (Li et al., 2021).

In order to further investigate the mineralization ability of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL, total organic carbon (TOC) analysis was carried out. As shown in Table S1, 65.2% of the TC molecules had been mineralized into CO2 and water after illumination for 2 h. It indicated that major parts of TC and its intermediate products had been degraded into H2O and CO2, and P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL exhibited excellent photocatalytic mineralization ability. Fig. S7 shows that the decrease of the photo-catalytic activity of P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL in five cyclic experiments was tiny, suggesting that P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL could maintain a stable degradation activity in long-term use.

A novel P-doped CD/Ni-MOL composite photocatalyst was synthesized by ball milling and hydrothermal method. The maximum degradation rate was 98.98% for P(1.0)-CD/Ni-MOL within 2 h under the visible light. The results of morphology showed that CD were semi-embedded in Ni-MOL. The physicochemical properties exhibited that P doping into CD could form C-P-O, C-PO and C-O-P configurations. P-doping modification could widen the band gap, form new band, reduce electron transfer resistance, form new electron transfer terminals, and improve hydrophilicity. The trapping experiments suggested that •O2 - played a crucial role in the degradation of TC. 65.2% of TC molecules had been mineralized into CO2 and water, indicating that P-CD/Ni-MOL showed good photocatalytic mineralization performance. This work supplied some inspiration for the establishment of P-doped photocatalyst.

Huifang Ma: Contributed to the conception of the study; performed the experiment.
